Um, but feel free to, um, introduce yourself.
We have 10 minutes with you, and
after you have finished, uh, speaking to your submission,
there might be questions from members.
Thank you.
Yeah. Please get started when you're ready.
Good morning, Madam Chair and members of the committee.
My name is Hannah Clo,
and I appear on behalf
of the Free Speech Union alongside Douglas Brown, one
of the members of our board.
We have made a submission on the telecommunications
and other matters, bill,
because it has significant implications for freedom
of expression in New Zealand.
For that reason, we oppose the bill.
Before I turn to the bill itself.
It's worth noting how broad the
telecommunications regime is.
A telecommunication covers almost any communication sent
electronically, uh, from texts, messages,
and emails to Fiverr and satellite data.
In short, the regulatory regime touches nearly all forms
of digital communication in New Zealand.
In the time available, I want
to focus on two provisions of the bill.
First, the bill is trying to apply New Zealand laws
and rules to companies based overseas
if they offer services to New Zealand.
These laws include consumer protection codes and rules,
but also require full interception capability,
meaning systems must be designed
and maintained to assist surveillance agencies
to obtain access to private communications.
This includes the duty
to decrypt communications if the entity
supplied the encryption.
Second, the bill introduces a new enforcement power,
allowing the Secretary of the Ministry
of Business Business Innovation
and Employment MBIE to suspend, revoke, or restrict radio
and spectrum licenses.
That means if an overseas company is unwilling
or unable to comply with our laws in relation
to the consumer codes
or surveillance, their license can be revoked.
If they don't have a New Zealand spectrum license
because they're piggybacking on another,
the secretary can then threaten to revoke that license
unless access to the overseas provider is blocked.
So what does that mean for free speech?
Well, the bill significantly expands the ability
of New Zealand surveillance agencies, New Zealand Police,
N-Z-I-S-G-C-S-B, to access private communications.
New Zealanders will no longer be able
to rely on overseas based networks
or applications, offering greater protection
for private communications.
Provided a warrant is obtained,
New Zealand surveillance agencies will have access
to communications across virtually all platforms
and networks used by New Zealanders.
This is dangerous because private encrypted
communication is essential for journalists protecting, uh,
their sources, whistleblowers, exposing wrongdoing,
individuals organizing politically or speak anonymously,
and members of vulnerable
or marginalized communicating
communities communicating with each other.
These forms of communication are fundamental
to accountability, dissent, and checks on government power.
Not only can they be accessed by surveillance agencies,
but any back doors into encrypted communications will be
abused by criminals.
Not only is the door being opened
for our surveillance agencies,
but is also being opened for anyone else
who can work their way in.
Now, that is just one free speech issue.
Content moderation
and platform governance requirements can easily be added
to these codes and rules,
which will be enforceable against all platforms
and networks, which the Secretary
of MBIE can enforce compliance with by suspending
and revoking licenses.
So what we're looking at is the creation
of a huge censorship tool by this bill,
which will be sitting there just ready to abuse.
And finally, we are a small country faced
with expanded interception, decryption,
and enforcement obligations.
Overseas companies may decide it's just not worth it
and withdraw, withdraw their services from New Zealand
rather than comply.
If that happens, the New Zealanders will lose access
to networks and services that they use for communicating
with people overseas.
Uh, access to diverse sources of information, research
and part participating generally in global intellectual,
cultural and political debate.
This isn't hypothetical.
This has happened and is happening overseas.
Just looking at the bill in the context of the other acts,
in the regime within the regime, the Telecommunications Act,
the Telecommunications Intersection Capability
and Security Act, and the Radio Communications Act, it is,
if it is even technically possible to achieve what it aims
to, it will have significant negative impacts on freedom
of expression in this country.
Now, that is an overview of our submission.
I will now hand it over to Douglas Brown, uh, from our board
to give him the opportunity to speak
and to take the lead in responding to any questions
that the committee may have.
Thank you, Hannah. So the real, uh,
focus that we have, or real concern that we have with this,
uh, comes through with the interception capability
that's being requested here, especially in regards to, uh,
overseas based service providers.
And that's really, uh, where this hammer's home is the fact
that the overseas service providers are almost all, uh,
these days offering what is called end-to-end
encrypted communications.
Governments around the world have been very keen to gain
what they call a backdoor, uh, what is, uh, called in the,
uh, interception Telecom, telecommunications, interception
and Communications Act,
or tsa, uh, an interception capability, uh,
into end-to-end encrypted, uh, communications.
Uh, it is mathematically impossible, and that is
because, uh, when you get a backdoor into something,
it is no longer end-to-end encrypted.
The providers do not hold keys between the devices.
They cannot interrupt the flow of the communication
if there is a way of interrupting that flow
of communication, uh, that is given to a government.
And that is what this bill is even essentially asking
those providers to do.
Uh, it is not just something that the government has.
It is something that any bad actor can discover and crack.
And we know for a fact that, uh, governments are not, uh,
the most advanced people in the room.
Uh, the bad actors, in fact,
are far superior in their ability to find those back doors
and to crack them and to use them.
Uh, we like to think that, uh,
our security services are in fact superior to the, uh,
foreign bad actors.
But that is in fact not the truth.
And we have found, again

have been cracked, uh, notwithstanding what is supposed

Provide us with this interception capacity.
